text: The technological landscape and applications of single-cell multi-omics. Alev Baysoy, Zhiliang Bai, Rahul Satija, Rong Fan. Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology. Abstract: Single-cell multi-omics technologies and methods characterize cell states and activities by simultaneously integrating various single-modality omics methods that profile the transcriptome, genome, epigenome, epitranscriptome, proteome, metabolome and other (emerging) omics. Collectively, these methods are revolutionizing molecular cell biology research. In this comprehensive Review, we discuss established multi-omics technologies as well as cutting-edge and state-of-the-art methods in the field. We discuss how multi-omics technologies have been adapted and improved over the past decade using a framework characterized by optimization of throughput and resolution, modality integration, uniqueness and accuracy, and we also discuss multi-omics limitations. We highlight the impact that single-cell multi-omics technologies have had in cell lineage tracing, tissue-specific and cell-specific atlas production, tumour immunology and cancer genetics, and in mapping of cellular spatial information in fundamental and translational research. Finally, we discuss bioinformatics tools that have been developed to link different omics modalities and elucidate functionality through the use of better mathematical modelling and computational methods. Introduction: Various single-omics methods have generated a plethora of single-modality data aiming to dissect the mechanistic basis of gene regulation or reveal aspects of human diseases. Methodological and technological advances now allow the simultaneous profiling of genome, epigenome, transcriptome, proteome and other (emerging) omics. Single-cell multi-omics technologies have been developed to directly characterize phenotypes that are usually inferred only from single-omics methods. As single-cell technologies have continually advanced, emerging methods capable of spatially mapping gene expression within the tissue drove the conception of 'spatial multi-omics'.

section: The Landscape of Single-Cell Multi-Omics (Part 1: Transcriptome Integration)
text: The landscape of single-cell multi-omics: The most mature of the single-cell omics methods, single-cell transcriptomics, is often paired with other omics. In 2009, the first single-cell genome-wide mRNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) method was reported. Building on scRNA-seq, multi-omics technologies preserve the progress made in characterizing transcriptomic information while simultaneously capturing other modalities and increasing throughput through cell multiplexing, cell tagging, cell hashing and other methods. Integrating genome and transcriptome: Parallel profiling of the genome and transcriptome of the same single cell can reflect the transcriptional state of the genome. The first amplification and sequencing of a whole genome and whole transcriptome from a single cell using a microfluidics-facilitated technology was reported in 2014. In 2015, genome and transcriptome sequencing (G&T-seq) and gDNA-mRNA sequencing (DR-seq) were reported. In 2019, TARGET-seq, a plate-based method, demonstrated increased throughput. Another plate-based method, simultaneous isolation of genomic DNA and total RNA (SIDR), involves incubation of single cells with antibody-conjugated magnetic beads.

section: The Landscape of Single-Cell Multi-Omics (Part 2: Epigenome, Proteome, and Other Modalities)
text: Integrating transcriptome and epigenome: Single-cell epigenome analysis in tandem with transcriptomics allows the direct elucidation of DNA epigenetic features. DNA methylation at CpG islands generally regulates gene expression. The first method of WGBS, single-cell genome-wide methylome and transcriptome sequencing (scM&T-seq), measured DNA methylation heterogeneity. Subsequently, DNA methylation co-profiling technologies were developed for tri-omics analysis. Chromatin accessibility paired with transcriptional analysis can reveal regulatory elements. Most recent single-cell multi-omics technologies that profile chromatin accessibility are primarily adapted from assay for transposase-accessible chromatin sequencing (ATAC-seq). Histone modifications at single-cell resolution are informative. Methods such as scCUT&Tag and scCUT&Tag-pro profile protein-DNA interactions. Integrating transcriptome and proteome: A great leap in throughput came in 2017 with the development of cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes by sequencing (CITE-seq). Similarly, RNA expression and protein sequencing assay (REAP-seq) demonstrated quantification of surface proteins. Other single-cell proteomics methods that do not rely on antibodies, like SCoPE-MS, produce a more comprehensive characterization. Other multi-omics modalities: scRNA-seq can be used in CRISPR screens (e.g., Perturb-seq, CROP-seq). Other modalities include the characterization of T cell receptors (TCRs) and B cell receptors (BCRs).

section: Multi-Omics Goes Spatial (Part 1: Imaging and Sequencing-Based Technologies)
text: Multi-omics goes spatial: Emerging spatial multi-omics technologies seek to expand mapping of interactions between single cells within intact tissues. Imaging-based technologies: Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) is a foundational technique. In 2014, a multiplexed smFISH approach was shown. In 2015, MERFISH substantially increased the number of RNA molecules. Sequential FISH (seqFISH+) can image mRNAs for 10,000 genes. RNAscope hybridizes DNA with probes tagged with metal. The spatial proteomics field has expanded with technologies like co-detection by indexing (CODEX). Sequencing-based technologies: The first in situ sequencing study used padlock probes. Fluorescent in situ RNA sequencing (FISSEQ) was the first untargeted in situ sequencing method. Sequencing-based spatial technologies moved from in situ to ex situ sequencing. In 2016, spatial transcriptomics was introduced (100-μm spot size), later adapted as 10x Visium (55μm). Slide-seq and Slide-seqV2 use micrometre-scale bead arrays. High-definition spatial transcriptomics (HDST) achieved subcellular resolution. GeoMx uses a targeted FISH detection method. In 2020, deterministic barcoding in tissue for spatial multi-omics sequencing (DBiT-seq) demonstrated co-mapping of tens of proteins and the whole transcriptome. DBiT-seq has expanded to spatial epigenomics profiling. Laser capture microdissection (LCM) is a decades-old method for isolating tissue regions.

section: Applications: Lineage Tracing, Atlas Production, and Tumour Immunology
text: Research and clinical applications of single-cell multi-omics: Tracing cell lineages: Recent high-throughput techniques have paved new directions. Transcriptomes contain information about cell identity. Clonal information can be encoded by DNA oligonucleotide barcodes. Technologies for profiling the transcriptome with chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, etc., provide new insights. Single-cell proteomics methods contribute to understanding protein level changes. The recently developed iTracer combines reporter barcodes with inducible CRISPR-Cas9. Combining genome, transcriptome and lineage reporter methods enables lineage tracing in diseases like cancer. Production of cell-type atlases of various organs: The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) is a cancer-specific multi-omics data resource. The Encyclopedia of DNA Elements (ENCODE) project aims to define functional elements. The Human Cell Atlas Project aims to provide an open-access resource integrating all single-cell omics data. Tumour immunology and cancer genetics: A newly developed spatial technology, slide-DNA-seq, was used to preserve local tumour architecture. Integrated analysis of transcriptomics and metabolomics revealed metabolic vulnerabilities. A combination of ChIP-seq and transcriptomics was used to detect enrichment of histone modifications. Study of the effects of SMAD4 inactivation led to identification of processes that may promote metastasis. Proteomics, genomics and phosphoproteomics were combined to classify hallmarks of lung cancer. These methods have also been used to study immune-checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapies.

Computational tools used for integration of multi-omics data: A major hurdle is the inherent complexity of data integration. Current tools can be broadly classified into matched or unmatched pipelines. In matched pipelines, various omics layers are measured simultaneously from the same single cell. In unmatched pipelines, omics layers are unaligned. Integration of unpaired data is limited by inherent information loss. A general solution is to project cells into a co-embedded space. Seurat v3 and linked inference of genomic experimental relationships (LIGER) are examples of unmatched pipelines. A recent unmatched method, graph-linked unified embedding (GLUE), achieves triple-omics integration. More recently, methods like StabMap, Cobolt, MultiVI and 'Bridge Integration' in Seurat v5 use separately collected multi-omics datasets to facilitate integration. For paired experimental methods (e.g., 10x Multiome, CITE-seq), different modalities are tethered to a single cell. Recently, Seurat v4 used a weighted nearest-neighbour analysis. Another method, DeepMAPS, uses a five-step framework. Methods like SCENIC+, functional inference of gene regulation (FigR), and probabilistic multimodal models for integrated regulatory analysis (MIRA) model joint variation to link enhancers to genes. CellOracle leverages multi-omics data to learn gene regulatory networks. Conclusions and future perspective: Single-cell multi-omics have become integral for elucidating biological complexity. Challenges include high sequencing costs, limited coverage, and the need for benchmarking computational tools. The emergence of spatial omics is transforming our understanding. Most current spatial technologies are low throughput. Finally, applying the dimension of time to single-cell multi-omics is necessary.
